Alright guys, JB here. Time for your throwback Thursday. Topic today is going to be scripting. I found a great clip of me explaining a certain element of scripting. So let's cut to that live footage right now and I will see you on the other side. You're going to really enjoy this. Scripts. A script is divided into four parts. Very simple. Okay? Point one is the introduction. The introduction. And this is that point on an outgoing call. Okay? If you were calling out, you'd say, hey John, you know this is so-and-so calling from XYZ company. How are you doing today? Great John. Well the reason for the call is whatever it is. This is your basic introduction. Don't worry about the particulars yet. It's your intro is how you-the first words that come out of your mouth. And remember there are-it's not a lot you can say in those first four or five seconds that can establish you as an expert. So it's very basic. It's a normal greeting. You know how you're doing today? Sound enthusiastic. It's more about your tonality. And that you care. You got to sound good. So it's-but again that's your introduction. Name your company and so forth. The second step is your body of your presentation. Okay? Now remember when I talk about scripting here and I'm actually-let me go back. I'm going to confuse you a bit. Because this is-let's just forget the intro in terms of when you first pick up the phone. Okay? Because there's this two types of-and I want to just go through the second one first. This-the intro meaning when-after your transition is over. Okay? Let me just reiterate this. So you've already asked your qualifying questions and you've gotten all the intelligence you need. Your product can help them and you've said, well John, based on what you said to me, this is a perfect fit for you. Let me tell you why. And we start from there. It's this part of the script I want to cover here. You have the same thing with the intro when you're first picking up the phone in the script. That's a separate script to cover. Okay? So you say the intro in this case would be about what your product is. Okay? We talk about what your product is in general terms. Name it the product, name it the company, name it the policy is, whatever it might be. Okay? And then from there you lead into the body of your script where you discuss the features and the benefits. And one of the-and I want to get into it so simplistic to say, you know, everyone's heard, you know, don't focus on features, focus on benefits, right? Well, the bottom line is this. You-whenever you talk about a benefit, you typically have mentioned a feature. There's always a feature with a benefit attached to it. That's the truth most of the time. But in the highest level of selling, the most powerful case you can make is not feature benefit case. It's through other ways of pattern storytelling and metaphors and comparisons. So I just want to just be aware that the body of your script, you're going to have a certain amount of it dedicated to features and benefits where you're making your logical case. And as part of that, body of your script, you're going to also focus on making the beginnings of an emotional case. So the first part's logical and you also want to tail off with emotion. Step three, you then will have what's called the close. So every script ends with you asking for the order in no uncertain terms. At a certain point in time, you have to say, John or Jill, this is what you should be doing. This is what most of my customers are doing right now. This is what I recommend. And you say, do this. You actually ask for the order. Okay. And then after you ask for the order and they either say yes or no, or let me think about it, right? For the people who want to think about it and you be engaging in what's called looping patterns and looping patterns are the mechanism that we use to take an objection and turn it into a close. How are you taking objection? We loop back and turn it into a close. Okay. And I'm going to explain this in intimate detail tomorrow, this process. Okay. So let me start off now. Let's talk about what are the elements of a great script? What makes a great script great? And one of some of the things that just kill scripts because there's some certain, the certain killers that just simply stop you from closing. Okay. Number one, the body of a script should be no more than a page or a page and a half. You don't want to front load a script. The biggest mistake I see that rookie salesman make is they feel that they don't get it all out in one breath. The person's going to say no and leave. And then the talking and talking and then they bring up every single great benefit. They do everything upfront. They shoot their load, so to speak. Right? And then when the person says, let me think about it, they're like humming, humming, humming, they're out. They're out of ammo. They don't leave any of their powder dry. Remember, the process of creating certainty in another human being is not a zero to a hundred mile. Someone doesn't go from being a three to a 10 in five minutes. It's not how human beings moved emotionally or even logically. It's incrementally. So you might get someone from a three to a seven and they slide back to a five. Then you give them another language pattern that brings up to an eight and they go back to a six and a half. Then the third when they get up to a nine or 10. So in other words, it's a series of moves that create absolute certainty. It's not one. The biggest mistake that salespeople make is when they do prepare, some of those that do engage in preparation, they don't go deep enough. They prepare for their opening can presentation and that's all they got. And it's usually way too long and front loaded with all sorts of features and benefits. Maybe some metaphors and storytelling as well, but it's so long when they finally are done and asked for the order and the person says, let me think about it like, oh, I have nothing left to say. They gave all their great stuff. Much more powerful is if you frame your case, you give some really great points and you build your case, but leave some great stuff out. So when someone says, let me, they want to think about it. When you actually engage in what's called looping, we go back and start reselling, you have even more powerful patterns to pull out of the bag, to change someone's mind. Like, oh, I didn't hear that before. You want to have some powerful stuff that they don't know about to bring them up to the next level of certainty. Remember when you ask for the order and they say, let me think about it, you're creating somewhat reverse consistency. Once someone says, let me, I want to think about it, they get anchored into I want to think about it, you have to overcome that consistency. Okay, but that's part of selling it. It's going to happen. We do that through deflection and by answering objections in a certain way. But the point is, is that you can't front load. You can't just say, if I say everything and I give him all my best stuff, then he'll say yes without ever saying no first or let me think, that's not the way it works. There's typically a certain amount of let me think about it. So let me call your back. So you have to go through till the guy says yes. So don't front load, leave some powder dry. Next step to a script. Every word in your script is crucial. I'm a stickler when I actually work on scripts for a company. I go through it in a painstaking process. When you read a script and you're reading it to yourself and when I say reading this, if you're selling over the phone, very often you can have it in front of you as just a guide, you know it by heart already. But as you're reading it, every word has to flow. If there's any tongue twisters in there, it's not flowing naturally. It's not a workable script. It takes a while to write, to develop a really good script. It's not a 30 minute exercise. It takes a bit of time. So you've got to dedicate that and when you're going through your script as you're reading, every word matters. Every word's got to be balanced. It's got to flow. It's got to sound good. If it doesn't sound good, it doesn't flow well, the script's not going to play out. When I'm going through my own script, I read it out loud to myself a thousand times. I keep reading it out loud. Is there any tongue twisters? Is it hard for me to say? How can I make it flow better? Every word in the script matters. Okay? Very important. Next. I want you to check in with the prospect often. Meaning you don't ever want to be taught talking for more than about 30 seconds to a minute without saying, you see what I'm saying? Does that make sense to you? You're following me so far? All good? Yes? Great. And start building consistently. Do not become a talking head. Where you just talk, talk, talk, talk, talk, talk and they're listening. They will tune out eventually, no matter how good you sound. So I want you to check in with the prospect often. Use transitional words and phrases. Meaning there are certain words in a script that allow you to catch your breath and they balance out a statement. So if I say to you, you know, it's Jordan calling from XYZ company. I'll say the reason for the call, I would never do that. I would never say, hey, it's called Jordan called XYZ company. The reason for the call, I have the word now. So now follow me. This is very important when you're writing scripts. These are the words that make scripts work. These are the transitional words. Now, actually, virtually, these are these certain words that allow you to catch a breath and they balance out. So I'd say, you know, here's how it works. I say, I would say, we spoke six months ago. I say now the reason for the now is my transition. You don't just say, you know, John, if you have a call, we spoke six months ago. I, you know, when I sent you out some information, the reason for the call, it has to be a now the reason for the call and that now allows you to catch your breath, to lower your voice and to change your tonality and sound good. Without the transition, it doesn't work. Believe it or not, it won't work. You sound weird because when we speak naturally, we use transitions all the time. All right, your back is remember the key always is strategic preparation. It's what you, what you do before you enter the encounter. How prepared I do. You know what you're going to say. Do you have those words all sort of in person memorized or you're scripted in front of you when you're on the phone? Remember, this is, this is like a gimme. In other words, why not prepare yourself? Why leave things up to chance in order to really use the straight line at the highest level? It can't just be about tonality and by language. It's about the words you say communication that's completely congruent from the words of tonality, by language all wrapped up into one bam, that's the close to the highest level. All right, love you all and we'll talk again tomorrow.
